🔎 Molluscum Contagiosum

Small pearly bumps that usually settle on their own.
Dr. Murali Gopal
Senior Paediatrician & Paediatric Pulmonologist
MCR: 57489
MBBS, DCH(UK), MRCPCH(UK), FRCPCH(UK), CCT Paediatrics (UK), Fellow in Paediatric Pulmonology (Aus), Allergology (Ind)
Last reviewed: 10 May 2026
Reassurance for parentsMolluscum is a common viral skin condition in children. The spots are harmless and usually clear on their own, although this may take many months.

What is Molluscum Contagiosum?

Molluscum contagiosum is a viral skin infection that causes small, smooth, raised bumps, often with a tiny dimple in the centre.

Symptoms & Signs

  • Small pearly, waxy, or pink bumps
  • Tiny central dimple in the bump
  • Often on the trunk, arms, or legs
  • Usually painless, but can become itchy or inflamed
  • May spread by scratching

Causes

  • Caused by the molluscum contagiosum virus
  • Spreads by direct skin contact and via shared towels, bath toys, or pool environments
  • More common in children and in skin that is already irritated

Home Management

  • Discourage scratching or picking.
  • Do not share towels, bath toys, or clothing.
  • Keep skin moisturised if eczema is also present.
  • Most children do not need active treatment.
  • Cover bumps if they are being scratched or in an area of frequent contact.

Red Flags / When to Seek Help

  • Bumps become very red, painful, or show signs of bacterial infection
  • Spots near the eyelids causing irritation
  • Widespread severe disease or concern about immunity problems
  • Diagnosis is unclear or lesions look unusual

Important Facts / Myth correction

Molluscum is usually harmless and self-limiting.
Spots often last for months before clearing.
Inflammation around a bump may mean the body is beginning to clear it.

Legal medical disclaimer

This parent guide is for education only and does not replace a medical consultation, diagnosis, or treatment plan. Advice should be interpreted in the context of your child's age, symptoms, and medical history. If your child has worsening symptoms, breathing difficulty, dehydration, unusual drowsiness, persistent high fever, seizures, or any emergency warning signs, seek urgent medical care immediately. All final guides should be reviewed by a qualified medical professional before clinical use.
